data.title: TCW Relative Value Mid Cap Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Environment

Indices in the U.S. and Europe hit records amid optimism over central bank easing, declining inflation, increasing optimism for a soft landing, and China stimulus. For the first time since 2020, the Federal Reserve (the Fed) cut interest rates doing so by 50 bps. After making its first cut (25 bps) since emerging from Covid on August 1, the Bank of England opted to keep rates steady at its latest meeting. The Bank of Japan also stayed put with its rate “around 0.25%.” The BoJ is the one major central bank in a hiking mode however is preferring to stay put until the new government is in place. To jumpstart its struggling economy, the Peoples Bank of China announced a broad stimulative package to combat slowing growth and waning investor confidence. Steps taken include cutting rates and incentivizing stock purchases and bank loans. In addition, the country announced a surprising fiscal stimulus package. These moves are major pivots from prior calls for “patience” and were reflected with strong gains in the Shanghai Composite.

U.S. job growth continues but at a slower pace. The August jobs numbers were below estimates and there were downward revisions to the prior months. The unemployment rate slightly dipped to 4.2%. Productivity was up 2% and the employment cost index softened, which should be supportive for corporate earnings. Wages are still higher than inflation and lower automobile gasoline prices are benefiting consumers. Recent U.S. economic releases (Empire and Philly Fed indexes, retail sales, industrial production, housing, and unemployment claims) were better than expected and are not recessionary indicators.

U.S. single-family housing starts spiked by 15.8% in August while permits were up 2.8%. Aided by lower mortgage rates, mortgage applications and refinance activity have risen noticeably. The Atlanta Fed GDPNow estimate for Q3 growth is ~ 3%.

With Q2 2024 earnings season complete, per UBS†, S&P 500 revenues grew over 5% YoY and earnings were up over 11% including stock buybacks. Big 6 Tech+ was the main force in Q2 up nearly 40% YoY but the group is expected to decelerate to just 21% in Q4 while the rest of the S&P 500 earnings growth is picking up. Earnings growth for both Financials and Utilities sectors were up double digits (+13%). Nvidia’s† Q2 earnings report disappointed even with a “beat and raise” as earnings fell short of analysts’ elevated expectations.

Sector Weightings1

For the third quarter, all sectors in the Russell Midcap Value were in the positive save for energy. The best performers were utilities (+18.0%), real estate (+16.0%), and financials (+12.6%) with industrials (+11.4%) and consumer discretionary (+11.1%) not too far behind. After energy (-2.6%), the bottom sectors were IT (+4.7%), consumer staples (+5.3%), and materials (+6.8%). Energy stocks fell in tandem with the commodity on hopes of reduced Middle East tensions though they may be tested with the recent increased hostilities. The portfolio’s sector weights detracted slightly with the benefit of being overweight financials and underweight consumer staples outweighed by the underweights in utilities and real estate and overweight in energy.

Security Selection1

Usually a positive contributor, stock selection detracted in the quarter. The portfolio’s top ten average-weighted names returned 15.0%, on average, better than the portfolio and benchmark index led by robust gains from Toll Brothers (TOL; 3.91%**) and Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL; 3.73%**), each with 30%+ returns. The portfolio achieved its best contribution from its real estate names which gained 21.7% outpacing the group return of 16.0% led largely by Jones Lang LaSalle and secondarily Welltower (WELL; 2.30%**). Homebuilder Toll Brothers and secondarily its peer Lennar (LEN; 1.39%**) highlighted in consumer discretionary where the portfolio’s stocks outperformed 13.9% versus 11.1%. The portfolio also benefited from its IT names.

On the downside, the portfolio’s industrials names returned 6.3% behind the group’s 11.4% rise with FedEx (FDX; 2.45%**) and Manitowoc (MTW; 0.19%**) largely responsible. Equitable Holdings (EQH; 3.03%**) was mostly to blame for the underperformance in financials where the portfolio’s stocks fell short 10.7% versus 12.6%. Acadia Healthcare (ACHC; 1.03%**) was the main culprit in health care. The company’s stock fell after a government probe into patient care. As a result of a fundamental review, the position was reduced by one-half.

Representative Buys (Ticker, Sector)*

Omnicom Group Inc. (OMC; Communication Services; 0.65%**)

MSCI ESG2 Score: Overall BBB

Omnicom Group Inc., headquartered New York, NY, is a leading provider of data-inspired, creative marketing and sales solutions. Omnicom’s iconic agency brands are home to the industry’s most innovative communications specialists which are focused on driving intelligent business outcomes for their clients. The company offers a wide range of services in advertising, strategic media planning, buying, precision marketing, retail and digital commerce, branding, experiential, public relations, healthcare marketing, and other specialty marketing services to over 5,000 clients in more than 70 countries. At initiation OMC had a market capitalization of $20.6 billion and met four of the five valuation factors: price-to-sales, price-to-cash-flow, price-to-earnings, and dividend yield of 2.8%. The fundamental catalysts are new products/new markets as the company continue to offer new creative solutions while at the same time expand its geographical footprint.

Teva Pharmaceutical (TEVA; Health Care; 0.91%**)

MSCI ESG Score: Overall BB

Teva Pharmaceutical Industries Ltd., headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel, with its U.S. headquarters in Parsippany, NJ, is engaged in the development, production, and sale of pharmaceuticals. The company is focused on generics, innovative proprietary products, and over the counter (OTC) medicine. At initiation Teva had capitalization of $17.1 billion and met four of the five valuation factors: price-to-sales, price-to-cash flow, price-to-earnings, and price-to-book. The fundamental catalysts are new management and new products/markets. Richard Francis joined Teva in January 2023 as President and Chief Executive Officer. With over 25 years of experience, his resume includes management positions at Novartis†, Sandoz AG (CEO), and most recently CEO of Purespring Therapeutics. He is committed to refocusing the company on new biosimilar products and building the TEVA pipeline of innovative products.

Western Alliance Bancorp (WAL; Financials; 1.53%**)

MSCI ESG Score: Overall BBB

Western Alliance Bancorp, headquartered in Phoenix, AZ, is a commercial and retail bank with 37 branches in six states with significant presence in faster growing states, Arizona, California, and Nevada. At initiation, the stock had a $7.4 billion market capitalization and met four of the five valuation factors: price-to-sales, price-to-earnings, price-to-book, and higher than market yield. The primary catalyst is restructuring as management has been making advanced preparations to become a Category IV bank years ahead of piercing the crucial $100 billion in asset threshold. The shares have yet to fully recover from the failure of SVB Financial, Signature Bank, and First Republic Bank in early 2023, and shares sold off again in sympathy when another bank was ill-prepared to be regulated as a Category IV bank; this bank took no preparatory action, and the regulators stepped in to force the bank to cut its dividend, raise capital, increase its reserves, and reduce loan concentration. By contrast, WAL’s management team had the foresight to hire consultants with regulatory experience, raised the floor of internal capital requirement to Category IV average, and purposefully slowed growth during the transition. Western Alliance ended the first quarter of 2024 with $77 billion in assets, but much of the requisite expenses have already been taken and we expect the transition to Category IV to be fairly seamless. It has already reached its capital floor of 11% CET1, with a broadly diversified loan book. WAL does have a below Category IV average reserve ratio of 0.74%, but appear justified against non-performing assets/total assets of 0.53% and net charge-offs/loans of just 0.08%. During the 2023 bank failures, which were in part triggered by massive deposit runs, Western Alliance’s management has been so transparent and communicative with its largest deposit customers, it has witnessed steady deposit growth, even during bouts of volatility. In the past year ended first quarter, loans and deposits grew by 9.2% and 30.8% respectively. We believe the company is now well positioned and poised for notable stock price improvement over the next 1-2 year time horizon.

Representative Sells (Ticker Sector)†*

Carlisle Companies (CSL; Industrials) MSCI ESG Score: Overall A

Carlisle Companies, headquartered in Scottsdale, AZ, is the leading U.S. supplier of premium roofing materials primarily serving the non-residential market as well as weatherproofing products and insulation for the residential and non-residential end markets. Over the approximately three-and-a-half year investment, the management team divested its non-core braking, fluids, and medical/aerospace interconnect businesses while expanding its wallet share of a new building facility by acquiring a company engaged in weatherproofing and sealants. These portfolio actions along with its lean manufacturing prowess helped CSL achieve its Vision 2025 EPS goal earlier than expected. Recently, CSL leadership formulated a new five-year plan to grow earnings at a 15% annual rate through 2030. As investors appreciated the company’s renewed focus as a pure-play construction products company as well as its strong execution against its five-year Vision 2025 plan, the stock appreciated to the point of meeting none of the five valuation factors, thus prompting an automatic elimination of the position.

Dollar Tree, Inc. (DLTR; Consumer Discretionary)

MSCIESGScore:OverallAAA(Environmental:AA,Social:BBB,Governance:AA)

Dollar Tree, founded in 1986 and headquartered in Chesapeake, VA, is a leading operator of discount variety stores. The company operates 8,415 stores under the Dollar Tree banner which offers merchandise predominantly at the opening price point of $1.25, and Family Dollar which operates 8,359 general merchandise discount stores at multiple price points. At elimination, the stock had a $14.6 billion market capitalization and met four of the five valuation factors as it does not pay a dividend. Catalysts for the investment were new management, restructuring, and new products/new markets. Former Dollar General† CEO Rick Dreiling joined Dollar Tree’s Board of Directors as Executive Chairman in March 2022 and was appointed to the role of Chief Executive Officer in January 2023. At Dollar Tree, management has expanded its multi-price product assortment to 1,600 stores to date. The company introduced $3 and $5 Dollar Tree Plus product in select discretionary categories and later expanded to $3, $4 and $5 frozen and refrigerated products in order to increase customer traffic and store productivity. In Q4 2023, management initiated a comprehensive store portfolio optimization review and identified approximately 970 underperforming Family Dollar stores, including 600 stores to be closed in the first half of fiscal 2024 and 370 stores to be closed at the end of each store’s lease term. Thus far, the company has closed approximately 655 stores and expects to close an additional 45 during the remainder of the year. In Q2 2024, it initiated a formal review of strategic alternatives for the Family Dollar business segment which could include a potential sale, spin-off, or other disposition of the business. The news initially brought optimism as the banner’s performance has created a consistent overhang to the stock price performance. However, the company’s inability to provide any visibility on its likelihood to successfully dispose of Family Dollar during the last earnings call, on the top of a deteriorating performance from the Dollar Tree banner due to a more constrained environment for the lower-end consumer and intensifying competitive pressures, led us to exit the position.

Evercore Partners (EVR; Financials)

MSCI ESG Score: Overall BB

Evercore Partners, based in New York, NY is an investment banking advisory and investment management company. At initiation, Evercore had a market capitalization of $1.8 billion and met two out of the five valuation criteria, price-to-earnings and dividend yield. The primary catalyst was new markets as the company augments its investment banking and advisory services for multinational corporations and private equity firms on large and complex transactions. Despite being on the cusp of a new mergers & acquisition cycle, the shares have appreciated to a point that it no longer meets any of the valuation factors, signifying it is no longer a value stock, as we define it. Consistent with our self-imposed rules, the stock was sold.

Market Outlook

While there are some cracks, the U.S. economy remains resilient and a standout among developed markets, and recession odds are at normal historical levels of 20-30%. With the Fed entering a rate cutting cycle, sentiment among buyers and homebuilders is increasing; mortgage rates have declined, mortgage applications have risen, and refinancing activity has gained momentum. Home Depot† has mentioned in calls that it typically experiences a pick-up in pro activity within two quarters after the Fed begins to cut rates. Alleviating concerns, at least in the near term, Congress passed a short-term funding bill which will keep the government funded through December 20. One near-term headwind has been forestalled with a temporary agreement ending the International Longshoremen’s Association (ILA) three-day strike that began October 1. Including the 33,000+ Boeing† machinists that are still on strike since September 13, the October payrolls number will be affected.

Inflation continues to cool across the globe. The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge (Core PCE) has fallen to 2.2% annualized for August. Singapore’s CPI in August fell to 2.2% YoY (from a high of 3.4% in February), Australia’s declined to 2.7% YoY, and though still somewhat elevated, Brazil’s annual rate of inflation slowed much more than expected with prices up 4.2%versus estimates of 4.3%.

German business confidence (IFO) declined for a fourth consecutive month in September and by more than expected adding to signs that the eurozone’s largest economy may have tipped into recession. “The German economy is coming under increasing pressure,” said IFO president Clemens Fuest while IFO economist Klaus Wohlrabe stated, “The German economy is on the edge of a downward spiral.”

Prices follow earnings. S&P 500 company earnings ex. Big 6 are poised to accelerate high single to low double digits into year-end while those mega cap names are expected to decelerate. Slower growth but no hard landing will be supportive for companies not already buoyed by AI infrastructure. Lower gas prices are a tailwind for moderate consumer spending and wages are still outpacing inflation meaning real purchasing power. Fiscal stimulus (2022 Inflation Reduction, 2021 Infrastructure Investment and Jobs, and 2023 Chips Acts) monies continue and are not expected to peak until 2026-2027, bolstering future earnings.

Fourth quarter is historically the strongest seasonally, especially in election years. The S&P 500 tends to pull back/consolidate in the fall but then resume its run into year end. Uncertainty about the upcoming presidential election (with polls suggesting a statistical tie) is creating some volatility, but in any given year the U.S. stock market experiences 3-4 5-10% corrections. Geopolitical risks abound (especially the heightened hostilities in the Middle East) which are likely to result in higher oil prices, but we believe the U.S. is a global haven for all asset classes. Our base case is for the U.S. equity markets to go up which historically occurs 70% of the time and rarely down in an election year no matter which party is in control. Even so, all portfolio holdings are stress tested for excessive leverage in case of a recession over the next two years and we completed a tariff macro stress test in the event of a Trump victory in November. Blackrock† estimates $6.5 trillion of cash on sidelines and $2 trillion in CDs which is likely to move as the Fed continues its rate cutting cycle.

Investors are likely to remain in a holding pattern until the election has been determined at which point they begin to position portfolios for the new administration. We are busy and actively working to make the best portfolio decisions possible with the information we have and will adjust should the “facts” change. We are constructive about the future but maintain strong risk controls for unexpected turbulent events.

Diane E. Jaffee, CFA, Group Managing Director

Mona E. Eraiba, Senior Vice President

MSCI ESG Ratings

An MSCI ESG Rating is designed to measure a company’s resilience to long-term, industry material environmental, social and governance (ESG) risks. MSCI uses a rules-based methodology to identify industry leaders and laggards according to their exposure to ESG risks and how well they manage those risks relative to peers. MSCI’s ESG Ratings range from leader (AAA, AA), average (A, BBB, BB) to laggard (B, CCC). Leader: A company leading its industry in managing the most significant ESG risks and opportunities; Average: A company with a mixed or unexceptional track record of managing the most significant ESG risks and opportunities relative to industry peers; Laggard: A company lagging its industry based on its high exposure and failure to manage significant ESG risks. ESG risks and opportunities can vary by industry and company. The MSCI ESG Ratings model identifies the ESG risks (called Key Issues), that are most material to a GICS® sub-industry or sector.

Glossary of Terms

Bank Loans – An amount of money loaned at interest by a bank to a borrower, usually on collateral security, for a certain period of time. Base Case – A term used to describe the highest probability outcome in the opinion of the presenter or author. It is often used in conjunction with describing other possible outcomes usually labeled “downside”, “negative”, “adverse”, “upside”, or “positive”. Basis Points (bps) – A unit that is equal to 1/100th of 1%, and is used to denote the change in a financial instrument. The basis point is commonly used for calculating changes in interest rates, equity indexes and the yield of a fixed-income security. Benchmark– A standard against which the performance of a security, mutual fund or investment manager can be measured. Generally, broad market and market-segment stock and bond indexes are used for this purpose. BOE (Bank of England) – United Kingdom central bank. BOJ (Bank of Japan) – Japanese central bank. Buybacks– The repurchase of outstanding shares by a company in order to reduce the number of shares on the market. Cash and Equivalents – Assets that include but are not limited to Discount Notes, Commercial Paper, Money Market Funds, STIFs, Certificates of Deposit, T-bills, Tri-Party Repo, U.S. Treasury and Agency Notes within 13 months to maturity, and cash. While management generally believes that most, possibly all, of the cash and equivalents would be available on a same day or next day basis under stable market conditions, we cannot predict the impact of any adverse developments in market conditions that might occur. Cash Flow – The movement of money into or out of a business, project, or financial product. CCC Rating – A CCC rating represents an extremely high risk bond or investment; banks are not allowed to invest in CCC rated bonds. CCC bonds are junk bonds. CD (Certificates of Deposit) – An FDIC-insured savings certificate issued by a bank to a depositor for a specified length of time. The bank pays a predetermined interest rate to the depositor for the duration of the CD. Central Bank – A monopolized and often nationalized institution given privileged control over the production and distribution of money and credit. Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) – A component of Tier 1 capital that consists mostly of common stock held by a bank or other financial institution. Consumer Price Index (CPI) – Measures the average change in prices over time that consumers pay for a basket of goods and services. Core PCE Index – Measures the prices paid by consumers for goods and services without the volatility caused by movements in food and energy prices to reveal underlying inflation trends. Corporate – Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. Dividend Yield – A financial ratio that shows how much a company pays out in dividends each year relative to its share price. In the absence of any capital gains, the dividend yield is the return on investment for a stock. Developed Markets – Countries that have sound, well-established economies and are therefore thought to offer safer, more stable investment opportunities than developing markets. Dividend – A distribution of a portion of a company’s earnings, decided by the board of directors, to a class of its shareholders. Easing – A monetary policy in which a central bank purchases government securities or other securities from the market in order to lower interest rates and increase the money supply. EPS (Earnings Per Share) – The portion of a company’s profit allocated to each outstanding share of common stock. ESG – Environmental, Social, and Governance. Eurozone – A geographic and economic region that consists of all the European Union countries that have fully incorporated the euro as their national currency. Expense Ratio – A measure of what it costs an investment company to operate a mutual fund. Federal Reserve (the Fed) – The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. GDP (Gross Domestic Product) – The market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given period of time. Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS)– A standardized classification system for equities developed jointly by Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor’s. Growth– A diversified portfolio of stocks that has capital appreciation as its primary goal, with little or no dividend payouts. Portfolio companies would mainly consist of companies with above-average growth in earnings that reinvest their earnings into expansion, acquisitions, and/or research and development. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Interest Rate – The proportion of a loan that is charged as interest to the borrower, typically expressed as an annual percentage of the loan outstanding. Leverage– The use of borrowed money to increase investing power. A firm with significantly more debt than equity is considered to be highly leveraged. Market Capitalization– Represents the aggregate value of a company or stock. It is obtained by multiplying the number of shares outstanding by their current price per share. Mergers And Acquisitions (M&A) – A general term used to refer to the consolidation of companies. A merger is a combination of two companies to form a new company, while an acquisition is the purchase of one company by another in which no new company is formed. Outperform– Outperform is when an investment is expected to perform better than the return generated by a particular index or the overall market. Since the performance of many investments is compared to a benchmark index, outperform refers to generating a higher return than a particular benchmark over time. Outperform also refers to an analyst’s rating on a security, and outperform is a better rating than neutral and worse than a strong buy recommendation. Overweight – A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. Price-to-Book (P/BV or Price/Book or P/B) – A ratio used to compare a stock’s market value to its book value. It is calculated by dividing the current closing price of the stock by the latest quarter’s book value per share. Price-to-Cash Flow– A ratio that measures the market’s expectations of a firm’s future financial health. It is calculated by dividing the share price by the cash flow per share. Price-to-Earnings (P/E) – A valuation ratio of a company’s current share price compared to its per-share earnings. Price-to-Sales – A ratio for valuing a stock relative to its own past performance, other companies, or the market itself. Private Equity – An asset class consisting of equity securities and debt in operating companies that are not publicly traded on a stock exchange. Recession – Two consecutive quarters of negative economic growth as measured by a country’s gross domestic product. Reserve Requirement Ratio – A central bank regulation employed by most, but not all, of the world’s central banks, that sets the minimum fraction of customer deposits and notes that each commercial bank must hold as reserves (rather than lend out). Standard & Poor’s500Index(S&P500)– An index of 505 stocks issued by 500 large companies with market capitalizations of at least $6.1 billion. It is seen as a leading indicator of U.S. equities and a reflection of the performance of the large-cap universe. Stimulus – Something that causes a reaction; an incentive. Stress Test – A simulation technique used on asset and liability portfolios to determine their reactions to different financial situations. Stress tests are also used to gauge how certain stressors will affect a company or industry. They are usually computer-generated simulation models that test hypothetical scenarios. Underperform – When an investment is underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Tariff – A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Technicals– Analyzing statistics generated by market activity, such as past prices and volume. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. Unemployment Rate– The percentage of the total labor force that is unemployed but actively seeking employment and willing to work. Valuations – The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Value – A fund that primarily holds stocks that are deemed to be undervalued in price and that are likely to pay dividends. Volatility – A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day-to-day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield Curve – A line that plots the interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality, but differing maturity dates. The most frequently reported yield curve compares the three-month, two-year, five-year and 30-year U.S. Treasury debt. This yield curve is used as a benchmark for other debt in the market, such as mortgage rates or bank lending rates. The curve is also used to predict changes in economic output and growth. YTD – Year-to-date. YoY – Year-over-year.

data.title: Market And Economic Insights From March 2025
data.description:
Introduction

Welcome to the SA’s The Macro Brief! This official SA Profile will highlight our analysts' latest economic and market analysis to help investors gauge the ever-volatile financial landscape through various recurring series.

The economy is the foundation of financial markets, influencing everything from corporate earnings and consumer spending to central bank policy and inflation. Understanding economic trends, policy decisions, and sector activity can be critical when assessing market opportunities and making informed investment decisions.

Check out these must-reads from March…

March’s Trending Themes

Trump, Tariffs, Trade Wars Part III

Trump Cares About The Stock Market More Than You Think | Lawrence Fuller | 3/12/25

“President Trump's erratic trade policies and contradictory statements have caused significant market volatility and investor uncertainty.”

The Tariff Tantrum Has Created A Buying Opportunity | Victor Dergunov | 3/17/25

"The market correction may be nearing its bottom, presenting a potential buying opportunity for high-quality stocks that have seen significant declines."

Trump's April 2 Tariffs: Everything You Need To Know | Danil Kolyako | 3/31/25

"The core idea of reciprocal tariffs is to impose U.S. tariffs on imports from a country equivalent to the tariffs that the country imposes on U.S. exports. President Trump stated on March 30th that these tariffs would initially apply to all countries."

What To Fear From Tariffs | MarketGauge | 3/31/25
